Name: Legal Data
LongName: Legal Interoperability of Research Data: Principles and Implementation Guidelines
Description: A cross-disciplinary legal and policy framework for enabling lawful, machine-readable, and harmonized access, reuse, and integration of research data across jurisdictions and institutions.
Subdomain: Research data law, policy, and governance
Aim: To improve legal interoperability of research data by promoting minimal-restriction reuse defaults, clear rights determination, balanced legal interests, transparent rights statements, harmonization mechanisms, and proper attribution and credit.
ManuscriptSubType: Institutional or governmental data policy guideline
Keywords: access and reuse of research data

Scope.Coverage: Cross-domain guidance documents and policy manuscripts that define legal conditions for sharing, integrating, and reusing research data, especially in public-sector and publicly funded research contexts.
Scope.Focus: Rights determination, legal-use-condition transparency, reusable rights statements, waiver/licensing strategy, justified restrictions, harmonization across jurisdictions or institutions, and attribution/credit norms for research data reuse.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not a study-design reporting guide (for example trials, observational studies, or systematic reviews) and not a technical file-format interoperability standard by itself; it is a legal-policy overlay for data access and reuse governance.

primary.description: Chen R, Uhlir PF, and the RDA-CODATA Legal Interoperability Interest Group. Legal Interoperability of Research Data: Principles and Implementation Guidelines. Version 1.0. 20 Oct 2016. Zenodo.

Checklist.section: Principle One: Facilitate lawful access and reuse

items.section: Principle One: Facilitate lawful access and reuse
items.item_question: Does the manuscript state that access to and reuse of research data should be open and unrestricted by default, or otherwise with the fewest limitations possible?
items.item_question_background: A least-restriction default is foundational for legal interoperability across datasets.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract explicit default-position language on openness, unrestricted reuse, or minimal legal constraints.

source_page_refs.quote: Guideline 1A. Access to and reuse of research data should be open and unrestricted as a default rule...

items.section: Principle One: Facilitate lawful access and reuse
items.item_question: Does the manuscript identify legal instruments that can place research data collections in the public domain without reuse restrictions?
items.item_question_background: Public-domain mechanisms reduce friction when combining datasets from multiple rights contexts.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract named legal mechanisms for public-domain dedication and who can apply them (government, institution, researcher).

source_page_refs.quote: Guideline 1B. Governments, institutions, or researchers can apply one of an array of legal instruments to place collections of research data in the public domain...

items.section: Principle One: Facilitate lawful access and reuse
items.item_question: Does the manuscript recommend CC0/PDDL waivers or similarly non-restrictive licensing for legal interoperability?
items.item_question_background: Waivers and non-restrictive licenses improve combinability of reused datasets.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract references to CC0, PDDL, CC-BY 4.0, or equivalent approaches and rationale for preference.

source_page_refs.quote: Guideline 1C. The CC0 or PDDL waivers of rights are the preferred... approaches... The non-restrictive ... CC-BY 4.0 license may also be considered...

items.section: Principle One: Facilitate lawful access and reuse
items.item_question: Does the manuscript require data access and reuse terms to be applied equitably across users, including disadvantaged groups?
items.item_question_background: Equity constraints affect practical and legal interoperability at scale.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract statements on equitable access obligations and non-discriminatory availability conditions.

source_page_refs.quote: Guideline 1D. ... data should [be] available equitably to all users, including the most disadvantaged ones.

Checklist.section: Principle Two: Determine rights and responsibilities

items.section: Principle Two: Determine rights and responsibilities
items.item_question: Does the manuscript require determining rights holders for datasets before dissemination?
items.item_question_background: Rights uncertainty creates legal incompatibility during sharing and reuse.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract procedures for rights-holder identification and pre-dissemination legal checks.

source_page_refs.quote: Guideline 2A. Research data disseminators need to establish who or what entity has the rights... before the data are disseminated...

items.section: Principle Two: Determine rights and responsibilities
items.item_question: Does the manuscript state that users must comply with applicable data rights and jurisdiction-specific user rights?
items.item_question_background: Interoperable reuse depends on reciprocal responsibilities, not only provider-side terms.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract user-side compliance obligations and jurisdiction-aware usage constraints.

source_page_refs.quote: Guideline 2B. It is also the responsibility of research data users to abide by the rights applicable...

items.section: Principle Two: Determine rights and responsibilities
items.item_question: Does the manuscript encourage policy and legal experts in research communities to participate in rule-making fora?
items.item_question_background: Practical legal interoperability improves when domain experts shape governing norms.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract recommendations for legal/policy stakeholder participation in governance processes.

source_page_refs.quote: Guideline 2C. Representatives of research communities... are encouraged to participate in fora that develop and implement laws...

items.section: Principle Two: Determine rights and responsibilities
items.item_question: Does the manuscript recommend structured education for researchers on legal rights and responsibilities in data sharing and reuse?
items.item_question_background: Training reduces accidental non-compliance and improves reuse confidence.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract institutional training or capacity-building requirements tied to data rights literacy.

source_page_refs.quote: Guideline 2D. A well-conceived educational process for researchers regarding rights and responsibilities... should be developed...

items.section: Principle Three: Balance legal interests
items.item_name: Waive public-sector IP rights as far as possible
items.item_question: Does the manuscript recommend waiving intellectual property rights in publicly funded research data collections as far as possible?
items.item_question_background: Default waiver in public-sector contexts lowers legal barriers for integrative reuse.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract default-position language on waiving IP and distributing data under least restrictive terms.

source_page_refs.quote: Guideline 3A. As a default rule, intellectual property rights in government or publicly-funded collections of research data should be waived...

items.section: Principle Three: Balance legal interests
items.item_question: Does the manuscript require governments or public institutions to justify restrictions beyond those in existing legislation?
items.item_question_background: Unjustified extra restrictions undermine lawful interoperability objectives.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract conditions requiring formal justification for additional access/reuse restrictions.

source_page_refs.quote: Guideline 3B. Governments and public research institutions need to justify any restrictions... over and above ... existing legislation.

items.section: Principle Three: Balance legal interests
items.item_question: Does the manuscript emphasize public interests when developing rules for access and reuse of publicly generated research data?
items.item_question_background: Public-interest framing is central to balancing openness with legitimate constraints.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract policy language that prioritizes or explicitly weighs public-interest factors.

source_page_refs.quote: Guideline 3C. Policymakers should consider public interests in developing rules for access to and use of publicly generated research data.

items.section: Principle Three: Balance legal interests
items.item_question: Does the manuscript advise reducing exclusive-use embargo periods to the minimum necessary?
items.item_question_background: Long embargoes delay legal interoperability and downstream reuse value.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract recommendations on duration and justification of exclusive-use or embargo periods.

source_page_refs.quote: Guideline 3D. ... should reduce time embargoes for exclusive personal periods of research use to the minimum necessary.

items.section: Principle Three: Balance legal interests
items.item_question: Does the manuscript advise against individual contracts or agreements that unduly restrict access and reuse of publicly funded research data?
items.item_question_background: Contract-level restrictions can override interoperability-friendly default policies.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract statements discouraging EULA-like or one-off contract restrictions for public-sector data.

source_page_refs.quote: Guideline 3E. ... rights holders ... need to avoid the use of individual contracts or agreements that restrict access to and reuse of the data.

Checklist.section: Principle Four: State rights transparently and clearly

items.section: Principle Four: State rights transparently and clearly
items.item_question: Does the manuscript recommend standardized, machine-readable statements of retained legal rights for research data?
items.item_question_background: Machine-readable rights metadata enables scalable legal determination during data integration.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract requirements for standardized rights statements and machine comprehensibility of legal terms.

source_page_refs.quote: Guideline 4A. The use of standardized electronic statements regarding the legal rights retained... can greatly assist ... including by machines.

items.section: Principle Four: State rights transparently and clearly
items.item_name: Use competent legal counsel for jurisdictional clarity
items.item_question: Does the manuscript advise rights holders to seek competent legal counsel when needed to determine applicable law and cross-jurisdiction differences?
items.item_question_background: Cross-border reuse depends on accurately interpreted legal context.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract recommendations about legal-counsel involvement for jurisdictional interpretation and conflict resolution.

source_page_refs.quote: Guideline 4B. The rights holder(s) ... should engage competent legal counsel ... to determine the applicable law(s)...

items.section: Principle Four: State rights transparently and clearly
items.item_question: Does the manuscript require rights holders to clearly inform users of any special terms and conditions of use?
items.item_question_background: Opaque conditions are a major failure mode for legal interoperability.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract explicit obligations to disclose special terms, exceptions, and conditions to users.

source_page_refs.quote: Guideline 4C. Rights holders should inform users about any special terms and conditions of use.

Checklist.section: Principle Five: Promote harmonization of rights

items.section: Principle Five: Promote harmonization of rights
items.item_question: Does the manuscript recommend using top-down, bottom-up, or mixed approaches to harmonize rights in research data?
items.item_question_background: Hybrid governance approaches can improve adoption and legal consistency.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract recommended harmonization approaches and when each is appropriate.

source_page_refs.quote: Guideline 5A. Research policymakers and practitioners can use both top-down and bottom-up approaches... to harmonize rights...

items.section: Principle Five: Promote harmonization of rights
items.item_question: Does the manuscript describe voluntary private-law bottom-up actions as useful but potentially fragmented harmonization approaches?
items.item_question_background: Recognizing fragmentation risk helps calibrate expectations for voluntary mechanisms.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract discussion of voluntary/private-law mechanisms and their strengths/limitations for harmonization.

source_page_refs.quote: Guideline 5B. Bottom-up actions based on voluntary, private law mechanisms... can be an effective approach... but are relatively fragmented...

items.section: Principle Five: Promote harmonization of rights
items.item_question: Does the manuscript describe top-down harmonization through treaties, agreements, legislation, or regulation?
items.item_question_background: Formal legal harmonization can substantially reduce cross-jurisdiction incompatibilities.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract references to hard-law pathways and contexts where they are useful.

source_page_refs.quote: Guideline 5C. Top-down harmonization through 'hard' law ... can work in some contexts...

items.section: Principle Five: Promote harmonization of rights
items.item_question: Does the manuscript recommend process tools (workflows, decision charts/apps, scoreboards) to operationalize harmonization?
items.item_question_background: Practical tooling helps organizations apply harmonization rules consistently.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract any procedural decision-support tools proposed to implement harmonized rights handling.

source_page_refs.quote: Guideline 5D. Process-based approaches such as workflows decision-making charts, decision making apps and tools, or scoreboards...

items.section: Principle Six: Attribution and credit
items.item_question: Does the manuscript recommend attribution of reused research data as a normative research practice, preferably not as a strict legal or licensing mandate?
items.item_question_background: Attribution norms preserve credit while avoiding unnecessary legal friction in data reuse.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract attribution and credit guidance, including whether positioned as community norm versus legal mandate.

source_page_refs.quote: Guideline 6A. Attribution of research data ... should be a normative convention ... and preferably not by a legal mandate...

Aliases: Legal Interoperability Principles and Implementation Guidelines
EvidenceExamplesPositive: We adopt the RDA-CODATA legal interoperability framework and specify rights statements, rights holders, and jurisdiction-aware reuse conditions for all datasets.
Our policy recommends CC0/PDDL public-domain dedication as default for publicly funded research data and limits restrictive contracts that impede downstream integration.
The guideline defines legal use conditions in machine-readable metadata so combined datasets can be reused without case-by-case authorization.
This framework balances privacy and national-security constraints with public-interest access while minimizing exclusive-use embargo periods.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: This paper proposes HL7 FHIR APIs for EHR interoperability and does not discuss research-data legal rights, licensing, or reuse governance.
The manuscript studies technical schema interoperability only, with no rights statements, legal use conditions, or cross-jurisdiction policy guidance.
A trial report includes a generic data-availability statement but no legal interoperability framework, rights-holder mapping, or harmonization recommendations.
Matching.Explain: Applies as a cross-cutting legal-policy reporting overlay when manuscripts or guidance documents address rights determination, licensing, rights statements, and harmonization needed for lawful interoperability of research data across contexts.
